did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Deep Learning (specifically a Deep Convolutional Neural Network or CNN) and Reinforcement Learning (Deep Q_Learning) to classify skin cancer types from dermoscopy images obtained from the HAM10000/ISIC dataset. The AI methods were used for segmentation (watershed algorithm), feature extraction, and classification tasks.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'Original Scientific Paper' in the title section. It presents a novel model combining Deep Learning (CNN) and Reinforcement Learning (Deep Q_Learning) for skin cancer classification, describes specific methodology (preprocessing, segmentation, training), reports experimental results (80% accuracy on the HAM10000/ISIC dataset), and details the contributions of the proposed work, which are characteristic of original research rather than a review.

title: A New Approach using Deep Learning and Reinforcement Learning in HealthCare: Skin Cancer Classification
journal: International Journal of Electrical and Computer Engineering Systems
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel skin cancer classification model that integrates Deep Learning (CNN) and Reinforcement Learning (Deep Q_Learning) to assist in early detection. The researchers utilized dermoscopy images from the HAM10000/ISIC dataset, applying preprocessing and watershed segmentation to extract affected areas. The CNN was employed to classify the images into seven specific skin cancer types, while the Deep Q_Learning algorithm was used to optimize the model's training and retraining process. The efficacy of the proposed method was evaluated using the accuracy metric, which achieved a result of 80%. The experimental results demonstrate that combining reinforcement learning with deep learning is an effective approach for skin cancer classification tasks.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as 'Benign or Malignant' in the HAM10000 dataset with 327 images used in the experiment.
Classified as 'Malignant' in the HAM10000 dataset with 541 images used in the experiment.
Classified as 'Benign' in the HAM10000 dataset with 1099 images used in the experiment.

Classified as 'Malignant' in the HAM10000 dataset with 1113 images used in the experiment.

all_task_types.target: Seven specific skin cancer types (actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, melanocytic nevi, melanoma, vascular skin lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the model classifies dermoscopy images into seven types of skin cancer using a Deep Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). The task is evaluated using accuracy (80%) on the HAM10000/ISIC dataset. Reinforcement Learning (Deep Q_Learning) is used to optimize the training process, not to predict continuous values.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (ISIC)
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset of dermatoscopic images obtained from the ISIC archive, containing 10,015 images of seven different classes of skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training the Deep Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model after preprocessing (resizing to 28x28 pixels, one-hot encoding labels) and splitting (80% of total data).
use.testing: Used for testing the model (20% of total data) to evaluate accuracy.
use.other: Reinforcement Learning (Deep Q_Learning) was used to train and retrain the model using this dataset to optimize performance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. The efficacy of the proposed method was evaluated using the accuracy metric, which achieved a result of 80%.

HAM10000 (ISIC).notes: The proposed model (CNN+DQN) achieved 80% accuracy using Adam optimizer with a learning rate of 1e-4 and 100 epochs. This result was obtained on the HAM10000 dataset split into 80% training and 20% testing sets. The paper compares this against baseline methods from literature, showing the proposed model outperforms DNN (76%), CNN (79.45%), and EfficientNetB3 (78%).

architecture_used.main_model: Deep Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) combined with Deep Q-Learning (DQN) for reinforcement learning-based training optimization
architecture_used.input_size: 28x28 pixels (resized from original 600x600 or 450x450 RGB images)

activation_functions: LeakyReLU (mentioned in context of similar architectures, but specifically Softmax for output)
Softmax (for the fully connected output layer)
architecture_details.segmentation_method: Watershed algorithm (used for preprocessing/feature extraction, not part of the neural network architecture itself)

training_details.reinforcement_learning_component: Deep Q-Learning (DQN) used to train and retrain the CNN model to optimize hyperparameters and find the best result

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer with Softmax activation

preprocessing_techniques: Data splitting (80% training, 20% testing) using Train_Test_Split
Watershed algorithm for segmentation to extract the affected area
conclusions: The proposed model, which combines Deep Learning (CNN) and Reinforcement Learning (Deep Q_Learning), achieved an accuracy of 80% in classifying seven types of skin cancer using the HAM10000/ISIC dataset.
The experimental results demonstrate that reinforcement learning can be effectively combined with deep learning to optimize the training and retraining process for skin cancer classification tasks.
The proposed CNN+DQN model outperformed several existing methods, including DNN (76%), CNN (79.45%), and EfficientNetB3 (78%).

The combination of deep learning and reinforcement learning is considered a powerful and robust tool for classifying skin cancer, allowing for decisions based on multiple results obtained through training and retraining.

There is potential for further improvement in the model's performance by supplying additional data using specific and clean datasets and exploiting the potential of deep reinforcement learning algorithms.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000/ISIC dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance (e.g., 6705 melanocytic nevi vs. 142 vascular skin lesions) and demographic bias (predominantly fair-skinned populations), limiting generalizability to diverse skin types.

The model achieves only 80% accuracy, which is modest for skin cancer classification tasks where dermatologist-level performance (>90%) is often the benchmark; this suggests limited clinical utility for high-stakes diagnostic decisions without further improvement.

The paper does not report standard deviation, confidence intervals, or statistical significance tests for the 80% accuracy result, making it difficult to assess the robustness or reliability of the performance metric.

The input images were resized from their original resolution (450x450 or 600x600 pixels) down to 28x28 pixels, which may result in the loss of critical fine-grained morphological features necessary for distinguishing between similar skin lesions.

The use of Deep Q-Learning (DQN) for training optimization is described as a novelty, but the mechanism by which DQN optimizes the CNN hyperparameters or weights is not detailed with sufficient technical depth to allow for reproducibility or critical evaluation of the reinforcement learning component's contribution.

No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely on a single 20% hold-out test set from the same dataset used for training, raising concerns about potential data leakage or overfitting to the specific distribution of the HAM10000 dataset.

The paper claims to be the 'first' to use reinforcement learning for this classification task, but does not adequately discuss or compare against other recent deep reinforcement learning or automated hyperparameter optimization methods in the literature.

The segmentation step uses the watershed algorithm, a traditional image processing technique, rather than a learned segmentation network, which may introduce errors if the preprocessing fails to cleanly separate lesions from background or hair artifacts.
